glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far. Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norivid) . The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them. Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) . Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . "It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales" ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008)

glossary.definition: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . glossary.definition: A measure of the computational resources required to represent and traverse the state space graph, which must be managed to avoid excessive memory or processing usage.

glossary.definition: A game state that is identical to another state in the same level of the state space graph with respect to all defining parameters, and therefore should be represented only once to optimize efficiency.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game. If there are identical nodes at the same level, they are not displayed several times, but only once per level, in order to save computational resources.

glossary.definition: searches for a solution path

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search process begins.

glossary.definition: The target node in the state space graph that the search algorithm aims to reach, identified when it appears in the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if the search goal node exists among the nodes located in the OPEN list. The search goal node is found; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: A path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node, corresponding to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem. There could be more than one solution path because the same search goal node can be reached by different paths that start at the same search start node.

glossary.definition: A node that has been generated but whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.

glossary.definition: Hill Climbing always selects the best node. The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes. Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing always selects the best node. The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes. Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: source of search principles
glossary.sentence: Backtracking gives answers to the questions about the repeatedly discovered nodes and cycles. The principle of selecting the next current node is determined by a specific search algorithm (Figure 16). Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking. The idea is very simple – if the search goal node is not reached by the current path, then it is necessary to backtrack to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and to try another path (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: a process of finding a solution path from a start node to a goal node within a state space graph

Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: Алгоритм расширяет текущий узел I, генерируя его три потомка - узлы J, K и G. Учитывая, что узел J уже добавлен в список CLOSED, он не добавляется в OPEN повторно. Узлы K и G добавляются слева в список OPEN в последовательности их обнаружения. Узел I перемещается в CLOSED как уже раскрытый узел. Узел K, находящийся на первой позиции в OPEN, становится текущим узлом. Алгоритм проверяет, является ли он целевым узлом поиска. Он является целевым узлом, поэтому алгоритм прекращает работу.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G. Taking into account that the node J has been already added to the CLOSED list, it is not added to the OPEN list again. The nodes K and G are added to the left side of the OPEN list as new nodes in the sequence that they were found. The node I is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The node K, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is the search goal node. Therefore, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: processus de construction d'une hiérarchie de clusters caractérisée par la fusion ou la division progressive des clusters, où chaque niveau de la hiérarchie est formé en combinant ou en séparant des clusters du niveau inférieur, avec un niveau minimal contenant un seul objet et un niveau maximal contenant tous les objets
glossary.sentence: La clusterisation hiérarchique est une méthode qui construit une hiérarchie de clusters en fusionnant ou en divisant progressivement des groupes d'objets, aboutissant à un arbre de regroupements successifs

glossary.definition: type de clustering hiérarchique qui commence par des clusters individuels et fusionne itérativement les deux clusters les plus similaires en utilisant une mesure de dissemblance minimale
glossary.sentence: L'agglomérative est une approche bottom-up où chaque objet initial est un cluster et les clusters sont fusionnés par paires jusqu'à former un seul groupe global

glossary.definition: type de clustering hiérarchique qui commence par un seul cluster contenant tous les objets et le divise récursivement en sous-clusters plus homogènes en maximisant la dissimilarité entre les nouveaux groupes
glossary.sentence: La méthode diviseive utilise une approche top-down en partant d'un unique cluster global et en le scindant progressivement jusqu'à ce que chaque objet soit isolé

glossary.definition: mesure de distance ou de dissimilarité entre clusters utilisée pour décider des fusions ou des divisions, où la dissemblance entre deux clusters est calculée en fonction des propriétés des objets qu'ils contiennent
glossary.sentence: La dissemblance détermine quels clusters seront fusionnés ou scindés en évaluant leur dissimilarité selon des règles spécifiques

glossary.definition: méthode de mesure de dissemblance où la distance entre deux clusters est définie comme la distance minimale entre n'importe quelles paires d'objets provenant de chaque cluster
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage utilise la plus petite distance entre un objet du premier cluster et un objet du second cluster pour déterminer leur similitude globale

glossary.definition: méthode de mesure de dissemblance où la distance entre deux clusters est définie comme la distance maximale entre n'importe quelles paires d'objets provenant de chaque cluster
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage utilise la plus grande distance entre un objet du premier cluster et un objet du second cluster pour évaluer leur dissimilarité

glossary.definition: méthode de mesure de dissemblance où la distance entre deux clusters est définie comme la moyenne des distances entre tous les paires d'objets provenant des deux clusters
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage calcule la distance moyenne entre chaque objet d'un cluster et chaque objet de l'autre cluster pour mesurer leur dissimilarité globale

glossary.definition: représentation graphique en arbre des relations hiérarchiques entre les clusters, où les nœuds (clades) indiquent les fusions et leur hauteur représente le niveau de dissimilarité des regroupements
glossary.sentence: Le dendrogramme illustre visuellement l'ordre et le niveau de fusion des clusters, permettant d'interpréter la structure hiérarchique des données

glossary.definition: nœud interne d'un dendrogramme représentant une fusion entre deux ou plusieurs clusters, avec une hauteur indiquant le niveau de dissimilarité à laquelle la fusion s'est produite
glossary.sentence: Chaque clade correspond à une fusion entre groupes d'objets et sa hauteur reflète la dissimilarité du regroupement

glossary.definition: méthode de calcul de la distance entre clusters, incluant single-linkage, complete-linkage, et average-linkage comme principales variantes

glossary.definition: ligne tracée sur le dendrogramme pour déterminer le nombre de clusters en interrompant la hiérarchie à un niveau spécifique, intersectant plusieurs branches verticales
glossary.sentence: La coupe horizontale permet de sélectionner un niveau de la hiérarchie pour extraire un nombre spécifique de clusters finaux

glossary.definition: méthode décrite par Pai (2021) pour implémenter le clustering diviseif, consistant à appliquer itérativement K-Means sur chaque cluster intermédiaire jusqu'à obtenir des clusters plus petits ou atteindre une taille minimale souhaitée

glossary.definition: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metric is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced.

It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. It is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive. In multiclass classification, precision for each class is calculated using the diagonal value as true positives and the sum of other values in the column as false positives. The macro average precision is calculated by summing precisions for all classes and dividing by the number of classes. The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: Recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. It is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. In multiclass classification, recall for each class is calculated using the diagonal value as true positives and the sum of other values in the row as false negatives. The macro average recall is calculated by summing recall values for all classes and dividing by the number of classes. The value of recall lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of recall lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives. After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: The F1 score is a metric used in classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. It is calculated using the formula: 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall). The value of the F1 score lies in the range [0,1]. A high F-score indicates that the precision and recall are high and close to each other. Thus, the better the model performance is. In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) . Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. It is calculated in the following way (Chapelle et al., 2006; Geron, 2017) : The value of the F1 score lies in the range [0,1]. A high F-score indicates that the precision and recall are high and close to each other (Chapelle et al., 2006) . Thus, the better the model performance is. In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall. Figure 4 shows the calculation of the F1 score for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: The ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots the false positive rate (x-axis) versus the true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of a hyperparameter. It helps visualize the tradeoff between true positive rate and false positive rate for different parameter values. The ideal case is represented by a situation when the curve reaches the upper-left corner that represents zero error rate on the false positives and 100% accuracy on the true positives.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) . The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) . The ideal case is represented by a situation when the curve reaches the upper-left corner that represents zero error rate on the false positives and 100% accuracy on the true positives (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve. It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0,1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5.
glossary.sentence: AUC can be calculated for each class vs other classes for the multiclass classification task and then averaged across all classes. It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0,1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron. A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron. Thus, the primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon. The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses. Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron. If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)). Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010). According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location. Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses. It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity. Synapses that are used often obtain an increasingly higher weight. For synapses that are used infrequently or are not active at all, the conductivity continues to decrease. This can even lead to them dying off (the fragment is adopted from (Ertel, 2011)).
